stem: On each of [[n]] independent trials, the probability of success is \( \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \). Let \( X \) be the total number of successes (so \( X \) is binomial). What is \( P(X = [[k]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This swaps the exponents on the success and failure probabilities.
This uses the wrong binomial coefficient \( \binom{n}{k-1} \) instead of \( \binom{n}{k} \).
This omits the binomial coefficient \( \binom{n}{k} \) entirely.
This uses \( k \) as the exponent on the failure probability instead of \( n-k \).
This uses \( k-1 \) as the exponent on the success probability instead of \( k \).
This uses the wrong binomial coefficient \( \binom{n-1}{k} \) instead of \( \binom{n}{k} \).

stem: On each of [[n]] independent trials, the probability of success is \( \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \). Let \( X \) be the total number of successes (so \( X \) is binomial). What is \( P(X \le [[k]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This is \( P(X > [[k]]) \), the complement of the requested cumulative probability.
This is just the pmf \( P(X=[[k]]) \), a single term, not the cumulative sum.
This is just the pmf \( P(X=[[k]]+1) \), the wrong single term entirely.
This is the MEAN of \( X \), an entirely different quantity from a cumulative probability.
This is off from the correct cumulative probability by a fixed amount.
This is off from the correct cumulative probability by a fixed amount.

stem: A certain process succeeds on each independent trial with probability \( \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \). Let \( X \) be the number of the trial on which the first success occurs (so \( X \) is geometric, taking values \( 1, 2, 3, \ldots \)). What is \( P(X = [[k]]) \)?
solution: The first \( [[k]]-1 \) trials must fail and trial [[k]] must succeed: \( P(X=[[k]]) = (1-p)^{[[k]]-1}p = \left(1-\frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]}\right)^{[[k]]-1}\frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses [[k]] failures before the success instead of \( [[k]]-1 \).
This gives only the probability the first \( [[k]]-1 \) trials fail, omitting the final success factor.
This swaps the roles of the success and failure probabilities.
This is the CDF \( P(X \le [[k]]) \), not the pmf \( P(X=[[k]]) \).
This is just the single-trial success probability, ignoring the earlier required failures.
This includes an extra, unjustified factor of the success probability.

stem: A random variable \( X \) follows a Poisson distribution with mean \( \lambda = \frac{[[lam_n]]}{[[lam_d]]} \). What is the ratio \( \dfrac{P(X = [[k]]+1)}{P(X = [[k]])} \)?
solution: For a Poisson pmf, \( \dfrac{P(X=k+1)}{P(X=k)} = \dfrac{\lambda}{k+1} \), so the ratio is \( \dfrac{[[lam_n]]/[[lam_d]]}{[[k]]+1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is just \( \lambda \), forgetting to divide by \( k+1 \).
This divides by \( k \) instead of \( k+1 \), an off-by-one error.
This inverts the correct ratio.
This raises \( \lambda \) to a power instead of dividing by \( k+1 \).
This divides by \( k+2 \) instead of \( k+1 \), an off-by-one error in the other direction.
This inverts the ratio and also uses \( k \) instead of \( k+1 \).

stem: A random variable \( X \) follows a Poisson distribution with mean \( \lambda = \frac{[[lam_n]]}{[[lam_d]]} \). Using the fact that \( \operatorname{Var}(X) = \lambda \), what is \( E[X^2] \)?
solution: Since \( \operatorname{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2 \) and \( \operatorname{Var}(X)=E[X]=\lambda \) for Poisson, \( E[X^2] = \lambda + \lambda^2 = \frac{[[lam_n]]}{[[lam_d]]} + \left(\frac{[[lam_n]]}{[[lam_d]]}\right)^2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is just \( (E[X])^2 \), forgetting to add the variance \( \lambda \).
This is just \( \lambda \) itself, forgetting to add \( \lambda^2 \).
This doubles \( \lambda \) instead of adding \( \lambda^2 \).
This subtracts instead of adds, a sign error in the variance identity.
This uses \( \lambda - 1 \) instead of \( \lambda + 1 \) as the second factor.
This adds 1 instead of \( \lambda^2 \).

stem: Let \( X \) be a binomial random variable with \( n = [[n]] \) trials and success probability \( p = \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \) per trial. What is the mode of \( X \) (the value of \( k \) at which \( P(X=k) \) is largest)?
solution: The mode of a binomial variable is \( \lfloor (n+1)p \rfloor \) (when \( (n+1)p \) is not an integer): \( \lfloor ([[n]]+1)\cdot\frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \rfloor = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This rounds up instead of taking the floor, off by one from the correct mode.
This reports the raw mean \( np \) without applying the floor from the mode formula.
This is off by two from the correct mode.
This is off by one from the correct mode.
This is off by two from the correct mode in the other direction.
This mistakenly halves the mean before flooring.

stem: A sample of [[n]] items is drawn without replacement from a population of [[N]] items, of which [[K]] are defective. Let \( X \) be the number of defective items in the sample (so \( X \) is hypergeometric). What is \( P(X = [[k]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by the total number of ways to choose the sample, \( \binom{N}{n} \).
This drops the factor for choosing the non-defective items in the sample.
This uses the BINOMIAL approximation (sampling with replacement) instead of the exact hypergeometric pmf.
This swaps which group contributes \( k \) versus \( n-k \) items.
This drops the factor for choosing the defective items in the sample.
This is just the population proportion of defectives, ignoring the sampling combinatorics entirely.
